description: Raw, emotional breakup song that moves from devastation to quiet strength
suno_tags: emotional pop, heartbreak, raw vocals, piano and strings, melancholic, female vocals, 80 BPM
vocal_style: Emotionally raw and exposed. Cracking vulnerability in verses, restrained power in the first chorus, building to full cathartic release by the final chorus. Slight rasp from emotion, not forced.

lyrics_guidelines.vocabulary_level: emotionally direct, visceral imagery, honest and raw
tips: Write from the gut — this is the 3 AM version of the truth
Use physical sensations to describe emotional pain (chest tight, hands shaking)
Chorus should be the one thing you wish you could say to their face
Bridge is the moment of clarity or acceptance — the turning point
Contrast past happiness with present emptiness for maximum impact

example_lyrics:
[Intro]
[Grand Piano]

[Verse 1]
[Soft]
Your jacket's still behind the door
I haven't moved it yet
The coffee mug with your lipstick stain
Is something I can't forget

[Pre-Chorus]
They say that time will fix it all
But the clock just stares at me

[Chorus]
How do you unlove somebody
When they're sewn into your skin
I keep reaching for the phone at night
Forgetting where we've been

[Verse 2]
I drove past our restaurant
They gave away our booth
A stranger's sitting where you sat
And laughing like we used to

[Pre-Chorus]
[Raspy]
I told myself I'm fine today
Almost believed it too

[Chorus]
[Powerful]
How do you unlove somebody
When they're sewn into your skin
I keep reaching for the phone at night
Forgetting where we've been

[Bridge]
[Whispered]
Maybe one day I'll stop counting
The months since you walked out
Maybe one day your name won't taste
Like everything I lost

[Chorus]
[Belted]
How do you unlove somebody
When they're sewn into your skin
I keep reaching for the phone at night
Forgetting where we've been

[Outro]
[Soft]
Forgetting where we've been
[Grand Piano]
[End]
do: Ground the pain in specific, concrete details — objects, places, habits
Build emotional intensity across the three choruses
Let silence and space carry weight — don't fill every moment
Use past tense in verses (memories) and present tense in chorus (ongoing pain)
Allow the bridge to shift perspective — from loss toward acceptance
dont: Don't be vengeful or angry — this is grief, not revenge
Don't use generic breakup phrases like 'you broke my heart'
Don't resolve everything neatly — real heartbreak lingers
Don't overdo vocal dramatics in the verses — save power for choruses
Don't make it so specific it becomes unrelatable — find universal in personal
